content.full_text:
Farn PolrrrcAl Pnacrtcns CouvttssloN
P.O. Box lt07 . -lltt J Strcet . Srcraurento. CA 95lill-01t07
(916) 321-.)660 . Fax (916) -121-0886
August 28,2000
James Scot Yamell
Atkinson, Andelson, Loya, Ruud & Romo
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 645
Sacramento, California 95 8 I 4
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-161
Dear Mr. Yarnell:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Board Member Jim
Ashwandan of the Galt Joint Union High School District regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r This agency's jurisdiction is limited to the
Act. (Section 83111.)
QUESTION
Mr. Ashwandan is the executive director of a nonprofit orgaruzation that promotes
agricultural education. Does the Act prohibit him from voting on whether to apply for a grant
under the Agricultural Vocational Education lncentive Program?
CONCLUSION
No. Mr. Ashwandan does not have a conflict of interest in the decision. The decision
will have no financial effect whatsoever on his private sector employer.
FACTS2
The Galt Joint Union High School District ("school district") offers agricultural
vocational classes to its students. To pay for these classes, the school district annually requests a
grant under the Agricultural Vocational Education Incentive Program. The governing board of
the school district decides whether the district will apply for a grant under this program.
I See Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at California Code of
Regulations, Title 2, Sections 18109-18996.
' These facts are based on the ones set forth in your request for advice and facts you provided in
subsequent phone conversations.


File No. A-00-161
Page No. 2
Mr. Ashwandan is a member of the school district's governing board. He is also the
executive director and registered lobbyist for the Califomia Agricultural Teachers Association
("CATA"). CATA is a nonprofit tax-exempt 501(c)(6) organization that derives its income from
membership dues. Membership in CATA is voluntary and is primarily composed of high school
teachers, community college instructors, university professors, and other professionals in the
field of agriculture. CATA is not a labor union.
As a salaried employee of CATA, Mr. Ashwandan manages the affairs of the
organization, engages in leadership development, organizes conferences and performs other
duties. Seventeen people sit on the governing board of CATA. No one op the goveming board
works for the Galt Joint Union High School District.
The Agricultural Vocational Education Incentive Program was created to enable public
school districts to purchase or lease agricultural vocational education equipment. The California
Department of Education awards and diskibutes grant funds to public school districts. While
CATA has lobbied the Legislature to support the grant program, the organization does not
receive any financial benefit from the progftrm. In addition, CATA does not lobby the California
Department of Education regarding any specific grant request.
AIIALYSIS
Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
A public offrcial may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualiffing
financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)
1. Public Official
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section 87100.) As
a member of a goveming board of a high school district, Mr. Ashwandan is a public official
subject to the prohibition. (Section 82048.)
2. Conduct Covered
The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or attempting
to use one's official position to influence a govemmental decision. (Section 87100.) These
terms are defined in Regulations 18702-18702.4. By deliberating and voting on whether to
apply for a state grant, Mr. Ashwandan will be engaging in activity regulated by the Act.


File No. 400-161
Page No. 3
3. Identifying Relevant Economic Interests
A public official has a disqualifuing financial interest in a decision when it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official,3 or on the following enumerated economic interests:
(1) AnV business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
(2) Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)
(3) AnV source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within l2 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
I
(4) Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
(5) AnV donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within l2 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).)
a. CATA as a Source of Income
Mr. Ashwandan has an economic interest in CATA as a source of income. Presumably,
he has received $250 or more in the last 12 months. (Section 87103(c).)
b. Members of CATA as a Source of Income
In some cases, when a nonprofit organization is a source of income to a public official,
we will pierce through the organization and attribute the income the official receives from the
organization to the governing members of the organization. (Lucas Advice Letter, No. A-99-
059.) The piercing rule only applies when one or a few individuals control the employment
relationship between the nonprofit and the official.
To illustrate, in the Wittlesey Advice Letter, No. A-97-552, a plaruring commissioner's
spouse worked for a nonprofit organization. One of the directors of the nonprofit organization
had participated with another person in the decision to hire the planning commissioner's spouse.
In that situation, we pierced through the organization and concluded that the director was a
source of income to the planning commissioner. On the other hand, in the Abt Advice Letter,
No. A-91-361, we reached a different result. In that letter, an airport commissioner was a salaried
president of a nonprofit organization. The entire 30-member governing board of the nonprofit
organization determined his salary. Under those facts, we advised that none of the directors was
3 A decision will have a financial effect "on the official," within the meaning of Section 87103, if the
decision will increase or decrease the official's personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her
immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) We call this the "personal financial effect" rule. Under your facts, the
district's decision to apply for a grant will not have any financial effect on Mr. Ashwandan's personal expenses,
income, assets or liabilities. Therefore, the personal financial effect rule does not apply.


File No. A-00-161
Page No. 4
a source of income to the commissioner because no single director controlled the employment
relationship between the nonprofit organization and the airport commissioner.
CATA has 17 directors on its governing board. All 17 members of the board make
personnel decisions regarding Mr. Ashwandan's employment. Under these facts, none of the
directors is a source of income to Mr. Ashwandan.
After identiffing his or her relevant economic interests, the public official must evaluate
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of those economic interests. This determination takes three steps, which we discuss
below as Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine whether the economic interest will
be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. @egulation 1S700(b)(4).) Then, based on the
type of involvement, the official must find the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. @egulation 187000)(5).) Once the official finds the applicable
materiality standard, he or she must decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard
will be met as a result of the decision. @egulation 18700(b)(6).)
4. Direct or Indirect Involvement
A person, including a nonprofit organization, is directly involved in a governmental
decision if the person initiates or is a named party in the decision. @egulation 18704.1(a)(2).)
In addition, a person is directly involved in govemmental decisions that involve a license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with that person. (Ibid.)
CATA is not a named party in the district's decision to apply for a grant. In addition,
that decision does not involve a license, permit or contract with CATA. Therefore, CATA is
indirectly involved in the decision for purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard.
(Regulation 1 8704. 1(b).)
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
a. Indirectly Involved Nonprofit Entities
Regulation 18705.3(b)(2) lists the materiality standards for source-of-income, nonprofit
otganizations that are indirectly involved in a decision. The thresholds in the regulation vary
depending upon the gross annual receipts of the nonprofit organization.
We cannot determine which materiality standard in the regulation is relevant to your facts
because we do not know CATA's gross annual receipts. As discussed below, however, this step
may not be necessary to complete the analysis.


File No. A-00-161
Page No. 5
b. Special Rule (Nexus)
Not'withstanding any other materiality standard applicable to nonprofit organizations, any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a govemmental decision on an official's source of
income is deemed to be material if a nexus exists between the decision and the official's private-
sector duties. @egulation 18705.3(c).) A nexus exists if the official receives income to achieve
a goal that the governmental decision would achieve, defeat, aid or hinder.
For example, we have advised that if an official receives income to advocate the policies
of an individual or group, he or she may not then participate in a governmental decision
promoting or defeating those policies. (Brue Advice Letter, No. A-93-336.) The rationale for
the nexus test is that when an employee eams a salary to accomplish wliat he or she does as a
public official, we presume that the employer is benefiting from the situation. Here, it is true that
Mr. Ashwandan receives income from CATA to lobby the Legislature forprograms such as the
Agricultural Vocational Education Incentive Program. However, you state that the school
district's decision to apply for a grant will result in no financial benefit to CATA whatsoever.
Therefore, the nexus test does not apply.
6. Foreseeability
We consider a material financial effect to be reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially
likely to occur. (Regulation 18706.) To be reasonably foreseeable, a material financial effect
need not be a certainty, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198.)
Mr. Ashwandan must consider whether it is substantially likely that the school diskict's
decision to apply for a grant will meet the applicable materiality standard in Regulation
18705.3(b)(2). We do not know which materiality standard in the regulation is the correct one to
apply. Nevertheless, since no financial benefit to CATA is reasonable foreseeable as a result of
the decision, Mr. Ashwandan does not have a conflict of interest in the decision under any of the
materiality standards.
If you have any other questions, please contact rne at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
qrf,iti&ll--,^
B{.J Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: Mr. Ashwandan is the executive director of a nonprofit orgaruzation that promotes
agricultural education. Does the Act prohibit him from voting on whether to apply for a grant
under the Agricultural Vocational Education lncentive Program?

CONCLUSION: No. Mr. Ashwandan does not have a conflict of interest in the decision. The decision
will have no financial effect whatsoever on his private sector employer.
FACTS2
The Galt Joint Union High School District ("school district") offers agricultural
vocational classes to its students. To pay for these classes, the school district annually requests a
grant under the Agricultural Vocational Education Incentive Program. The governing board of
the school district decides whether the district will apply for a grant under this program.
I See, Title 2, Sections 18109-18996.
' These facts are based on the ones set forth in your request for advice and facts you provided in
subsequent phone conversations.
Mr. Ashwandan is a member of the school district's governing board. He is also the
executive director and registered lobbyist for the Califomia Agricultural Teachers Association
("CATA"). CATA is a nonprofit tax-exempt 501(c)(6) organization that derives its income from
membership dues. Membership in CATA is voluntary and is primarily composed of high school
teachers, community college instructors, university professors, and other professionals in the
field of agriculture. CATA is not a labor union.
As a salaried employee of CATA, Mr. Ashwandan manages the affairs of the
organization, engages in leadership development, organizes conferences and performs other
duties. Seventeen people sit on the governing board of CATA. No one op the goveming board
works for the Galt Joint Union High School District.
The Agricultural Vocational Education Incentive Program was created to enable public
school districts to purchase or lease agricultural vocational education equipment. The California
Department of Education awards and diskibutes grant funds to public school districts. While
CATA has lobbied the Legislature to support the grant program, the organization does not
receive any financial benefit from the progftrm. In addition, CATA does not lobby the California
Department of Education regarding any specific grant request.
